content:
Ryan, that kind of swap doesn’t just change a sentence; it trains a soul. When a man habitually turns “the windows of heaven were opened” into “a meteorological condensation event,” he’s choosing a different kind of world to live in.

What it does to him
- It flattens reality. God’s personal action becomes an impersonal process. Rain ceases to be a gift from a Father (Matthew 5:45) and becomes only a system.
- It weakens his ear for Scripture. The Bible’s native tongue is doxology, metaphor, and testimony. If he mistrusts that tongue, eventually he mistrusts the tone of the whole book.
- It dislodges primary from secondary causes. Scripture says God opens, sends, commands (Psalm 78:23; Job 38:25–28). Science describes the means. Reducing everything to “means” makes God feel unnecessary.
- It shrinks prayer. If weather is only weather, why pray for rain? Elijah did (James 5:17–18). Jesus says the Father sends it. A mechanized world silences petitions and thanksgiving.

What it costs him over time
- Awe and worship. Poetry is the porch of praise. If he can’t say “You opened the heavens,” his heart has fewer doors into wonder.
- Comfort and courage. In storms—literal or personal—he will have terms, not a Shepherd. Mechanisms don’t keep watch; a Father does.
- Moral seriousness. Sin becomes “maladjustment,” providence becomes “chance,” miracles become “low-probability events.” Repentance and trust feel unnecessary.
- Communion with the church. The Psalms become foreign. He will struggle to sing what Scripture sings and therefore to feel what Scripture feels.
- Inheritance for his children. They will catch his tone. If he speaks a thin world, they’ll inhabit a thin world.

A word on inerrancy and genre
- Calling them “windows of heaven” is not error; it’s the right register. The Bible names God as the living Actor who “upholds all things by the word of his power” (Hebrews 1:3; Colossians 1:17). It tells the truth about agency. Barometers tell the truth about process. Both can be affirmed without replacing one with the other.

A better habit
- Learn to say both, in the right order. First: “The Lord opened the heavens.” Then: “He did it through this front and those clouds.” Let secondary causes lead you to the First Cause, not away from Him.
- Let Scripture tutor your tongue. Pray Psalm 104 when you hear thunder. Read Job 38–39 when the forecast changes. Thank the Father out loud when rain comes (Deuteronomy 11:14; Matthew 5:45).
- Guard against cynicism. When you feel the itch to flatten, answer it with praise: “From your hand, Father.” Keep biblical metaphors alive in your prayers, your speech at the table, and your instruction to your kids.
- Ask again. Pray for rain, for relief, for seasons, because God commands every drop as He uses the means He made.

In short, that translation costs a man a sense of living under a Father’s sky. Over time, it hollows out wonder, prayer, and the ability to read the Bible as it means to be read. Keep the heavens open in your language, Ryan, and your heart will stay open to the God who opens them.
